coredata.dc:title: Structure optimization and performance of piezoelectric energy harvester for improving road power generation effect
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chaohui
Zhou, Ruoling
Wang, Shuai
Yuan, Huazhi
Cao, Hongyun
coredata.dc:description: This paper presents a cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester with excellent electrical output and mechanical characteristics for roads, which solves the problems of low electrical output level and low matching with road traffic of cantilever piezoelectric energy harvesters at present. Based on the finite element model, the structural characteristics of the energy harvester under different constraint forms are analyzed. The energy harvester structure is optimized according to the electrical output and stress distribution characteristics, and the size with high electrical output and good stress distribution is clarified. Finally, the good electrical effect of the proposed energy harvester structure and size is determined. The results indicate that the rigidly constrained energy harvester has higher potential and stress of the piezoelectric layer than the cantilever constrained, but the charge cancellation at both ends of the piezoelectric layer is obvious. Then the fixed end position and the displacement application position are considered to alleviate this phenomenon, and the former has obvious benefits in improving the electrical output effect and reducing the stress value. From the perspective of mechanics and electricity, the output voltage and output power of the energy harvester under the optimal size up to 24 V and 4.381 mW respectively, which are higher than other cantilever energy harvesters. The optimized energy harvester improves its electrical effect and mechanical characteristics under actual road traffic conditions, which lays a foundation for the cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester to collect road vibration energy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Energy-harvesting
Cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester
Electrical output
Stress distribution
Road engineering

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst misctext primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Establishment and verification of finite element model
Structural optimization of cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester
Optimization based on displacement application region
Size design based on mechanical-electrical performance

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Innovation Capability Support Program of Shaanxi
Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This paper describes research activities mainly requested and sponsored by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, China (No. 300102212910 ), Innovation Capability Support Program of Shaanxi (No. 2022TD-07 ) and “Hongliu Excellent Young” Talents Support Program of Lanzhou University of Technology, China . The sponsorship and team's efforts are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis process of rectangular cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester. (a) Simplified diagram [14] (Copyright 2020, Elsevier); (b) Deformation analysis diagram.

Establishment and verification of finite element model. (a) Finite element model of cantilever constraint; (b) Finite element model of rigid constraint; (c) Comparison of theoretical and finite element results of energy harvesters with different sizes.

Output power, potential distribution, and stress distribution of energy harvesters under different constraints. (a) Output power of cantilever constraint; (b) Output power of rigid constraint; (c) Potential distribution of cantilever constraint; (d) Potential distribution of rigid constraint; (e) Stress distribution of cantilever constraint; (f) Stress distribution of rigid constraint.

Output power and stress analysis with different distances between the piezoelectric layer and fixed end. (a) Output power; (b) Maximum output power; (c) Stress analysis; (d) Comparison between maximum output power and maximum stress.

Output power and stress analysis under different horizontal distances of piezoelectric layers. (a) Output power; (b) Maximum output power; (c) Stress analysis; (d) Comparison between maximum output power and maximum stress.
Effect of the length and width on the electrical output and stress. (a) Length; (b) Width.
Effect of the length under similar piezoelectric layer stress. (a) Electrical performance; (b) Mechanical performance; (c) Comprehensive electrical index.
The influence of the width on the performance. (a) Electrical performance; (b) Mechanical performance.
Electrical performance test. (a) Terminal voltage; (b) Voltage under optimum load; (c) Output power; (d) Power density.
Material parameters of finite element model.
Principal component eigenvalue and contribution rate.

head.ce:title: Structure optimization and performance of piezoelectric energy harvester for improving road power generation effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Highway, Chang'an University, Xi'an, 710064, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Highway, Chang’an University, Xi’an, 710064, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil Engineering, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou, 730050, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Civil Engineering, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou, 730050, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper presents a cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester with excellent electrical output and mechanical characteristics for roads, which solves the problems of low electrical output level and low matching with road traffic of cantilever piezoelectric energy harvesters at present. Based on the finite element model, the structural characteristics of the energy harvester under different constraint forms are analyzed. The energy harvester structure is optimized according to the electrical output and stress distribution characteristics, and the size with high electrical output and good stress distribution is clarified. Finally, the good electrical effect of the proposed energy harvester structure and size is determined. The results indicate that the rigidly constrained energy harvester has higher potential and stress of the piezoelectric layer than the cantilever constrained, but the charge cancellation at both ends of the piezoelectric layer is obvious. Then the fixed end position and the displacement application position are considered to alleviate this phenomenon, and the former has obvious benefits in improving the electrical output effect and reducing the stress value. From the perspective of mechanics and electricity, the output voltage and output power of the energy harvester under the optimal size up to 24 V and 4.381 mW respectively, which are higher than other cantilever energy harvesters. The optimized energy harvester improves its electrical effect and mechanical characteristics under actual road traffic conditions, which lays a foundation for the cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester to collect road vibration energy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: With the depletion of traditional fossil fuels and the increasingly serious ecological and environmental problems, the development of green and high-performance materials or technologies has become an inevitable trend in the development of modern low-carbon society [1,2], in which the production and collection of clean, green and renewable energy are increasingly important [3]. In addition to traditional new energy such as solar energy and wind energy, more and more attention has been paid to converting road mechanical energy into clean electrical energy through piezoelectric power generation technology for roads [4–6]. As the energy conversion core of this technology, cantilever piezoelectric energy harvesters which have been studied more have attracted much attention, because they can adapt to low-frequency vibration environments and have high force-electricity conversion efficiency [7,8].

At present, the research on cantilever piezoelectric energy harvesters focuses on the design of piezoelectric energy harvesters [9,10] and the development and performance testing of devices [11,12], which have promoted the integration of piezoelectric power generation technology and road engineering applications. In the design of energy harvesters, researchers have improved their structures and reduced their resonance frequency to make them more suitable for low-frequency road conditions. For example, Chen N et al. simulated and analyzed the electrical properties of a PZT-PZN double crystal trapezoidal piezoelectric cantilever beam, which can achieve 4220 W/m3 power density at 5.6 Hz resonance frequency [13]. Wang S et al. proposed a multi-layer cantilever piezoelectric energy conversion structure for the pavement with a 5.2 V output voltage and a 6300 W/m3 power density at 5 Hz-4 mm [14]. Park S et al. used the output voltage and voltage robustness as indicators to optimize the size of the piezoelectric energy harvesters based on the Taguchi methods, the output voltage of which with a size of 30 mm × 60 mm × 0.3 mm is close to 22 V at 5 Hz resonant frequency [15]. Gu developed a cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester suitable for low-frequency environmental vibration energy harvesting, the output voltage of which with a size of 100 mm × 10 mm × 1 mm can reach 35.8 V under the condition of 0.4 g acceleration and 5 Hz [16]. These studies have made some contributions to the design of piezoelectric energy harvesters, but the structure and size are poorly matched with road application conditions so their electrical output effects still do not meet the application requirements of road engineering.

In device development and performance testing, the overall effect of piezoelectric power generation devices can reflect the electrical properties of a single piezoelectric energy harvester. Kim K et al. designed a piezoelectric power generation device composed of 80 cantilever piezoelectric energy harvesters in parallel, which can convert the small vertical displacement generated by the vehicle rolling into electric energy and the maximum output power of a single piezoelectric energy harvester can reach 10.38 mW under the excitation condition of 15 Hz-2 mm [17,18]. Do Hong S et al. designed a 33.8 cm × 38.6 cm × 10 cm cantilever beam piezoelectric power generation device with 120 parallel piezoelectric energy harvesters integrated inside and the maximum output power of a single energy harvester is 8.26 mW under the test condition of 2 mm–15 Hz [19]. Wang S et al. proposed a batch processing cantilever piezoelectric device suitable for road traffic conditions, and when simulating the conventional speed of 50–90 km/h (7–11 Hz) light traffic, the electrical effect of a single device is the best with the 26 V-1.10 mW maximum power output of a single energy harvester, but the power under large traffic flow is relatively poor with 20.8 V-0.40 mW [8]. By comparing the electrical output effect of a single energy harvester, it can be found that its structure and size still do not meet the road traffic application conditions. Piezoelectric energy harvesters with high electrical output levels need to be further developed.

These studies have improved the road applicability of cantilever piezoelectric energy harvesters to a certain extent, but there is still a gap in the actual application in the aspect of electricity and mechanics. The reason for this gap is mainly related to the lack of matching of road traffic characteristics. In a complex road traffic environment, the constraint form, action structure, and component size of the piezoelectric energy harvester will affect the structural deformation characteristics of its working state, and then affect the effect of converting road mechanical energy into clean electrical energy. Therefore, it is necessary to optimize the structure and size of the piezoelectric energy harvester in line with the road traffic characteristics by some means, to solve the problem of low level of the electrical output of cantilever piezoelectric energy harvesters and low matching with road traffic.

In this paper, the electrical theory and finite element model of the cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester are established, and the structural characteristics of energy harvesters under cantilever and rigid constraints are analyzed. The energy harvester structure with high electrical output and good stress distribution is optimized based on the two optimization methods of fixed end and displacement application area, and the piezoelectric layer size and substrate size corresponding to the best mechanical-electrical performance are determined. Finally, the excellent electrical output effect of the proposed energy harvester structure is verified through testing and comparison, which lays a foundation for cantilever piezoelectric energy harvesters to collect vibration energy in the actual road area.

The cantilever beam piezoelectric energy harvester consists of the substrate and piezoelectric ceramic plates. The analysis process of the rectangular cantilever beam piezoelectric energy harvester is simplified in Fig. 1 by ignoring the influence of the adhesive.

To further improve the electrical output effect of the cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester, the key parameters affecting its power generation performance are defined based on the power generation theory. The bending moment
M
and curvature
k
are expressed as follows:
(1)

Where
s 11 E
is the compliance coefficient of piezoelectric materials,
ε 33 T
is the dielectric constant of piezoelectric materials,
d 31
is the piezoelectric coefficient of piezoelectric materials,
F
is the applied force at the end of the energy harvester,
l
is the length of the energy harvester,
b
is the width of the energy harvester,
h m
is the thickness of the energy harvester substrate,
h p
is the thickness of the piezoelectric layer of the energy harvester, θ is the radian, L is the arc length of the lower piezoelectric layer,
L ′
is the arc length of the upper piezoelectric layer, y is the distance from the force to the neutral axis,
R ′
is the radius of curvature.

Eq. (3) shows that the length, width, and thickness of the piezoelectric layer and substrate thickness of the rectangular energy harvester can affect its electrical output effect. Therefore, the length, width, thickness of the piezoelectric layer, substrate thickness, and other factors should be fully considered when the size of the energy harvester is optimized to maximize the electrical output.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment and verification of finite element model
ce:section.ce:para:
The constraint form of a cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester is an important factor affecting its energy output. The energy harvester under cantilever constraint and rigid constraint has good stability, which is consistent with the characteristics of high-frequency compression of road structures. Therefore, cantilever constraint and rigid constraint are selected as the constraint forms, and the finite element models are established respectively.
(1)
Model establishment

When establishing the finite element model, the influence of the binder is ignored, and the structure of the energy harvester is simplified as “piezoelectric ceramics + substrate”. To better fit the actual stress state, two fixed blocks are added at the fixed end to clamp the substrate, and a cylindrical force carrier is added at the acting end to apply force or displacement excitation to the energy harvester. PZT-5H, which has high compressive strength, high piezoelectric coefficient, high dielectric constant, and meets the high electrical output requirements of piezoelectric energy harvester, is selected as the piezoelectric ceramic material. Brass with high strength and good durability is selected as the substrate material, and structural steel is selected as the fixing block and force transmission carrier. The specific parameters of the materials are shown in Table 1 .

The cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester has a regular shape, so the grid can be divided by mapping + sweeping. The finite element model and meshing are shown in Fig. 2 (a and b). The meshing quality directly affects the accuracy of the finite element model. High-quality meshing helps to accurately analyze the working state of the cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester. By analyzing the meshing quality of the finite element model, it can be seen that the average unit mass is as high as 0.939 and 0.950 respectively after meshing the finite element model by mapping + sweeping method, which indicates the superiority of the divided grid.
(2)
Model verification

To verify the correctness of the established finite element model, the two-way verification of the theory and finite element is adopted. Since the output power is derived from the voltage, the voltage is used here as the verification index, and the single factor change is controlled during the verification. The initial dimensions are 50 mm in length, 30 mm in width, 0.3 mm in thickness of the piezoelectric layer, and 0.3 mm in substrate thickness. The verification results are shown in Fig. 2 (c). The theoretical calculation value and the finite element simulation result are basically consistent when the size factors change. In most cases, the error between the finite element voltage value and the theoretical calculation value is only about 1%, and the maximum error is only 6%, which verifies the correctness of the established finite element model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural optimization of cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester
ce:section.ce:para: Under different constraint forms and displacement action forms, the energy harvester has different stress states and mechanical-electrical conversion effects. It is necessary to optimize the best constraint and displacement action structure of the energy harvester according to its mechanical-electrical performance.

After the model verification of the energy harvester with cantilever and rigid constraint, the constraint form with the best performance is further optimized based on the electrical output and stress distribution.
(1)
Optimization based on electrical output

The output power of the energy harvester determines the power output to a certain extent. Therefore, the conditions of 3 Hz-1 mm, 6 Hz-1 mm, and 10 Hz-1 mm are applied to analyze the power output characteristics under different excitation frequencies and external resistance values, as shown in Fig. 3 (a and b). The trends of the output power of energy harvesters with the cantilever and rigid constraint are similar. However, at the same excitation frequency, the output power of the rigidly constrained energy harvester is significantly lower than that of the cantilever constrained.

To analyze the reason, the potential distribution cloud diagram of the energy harvester under the two constraint conditions is drawn, as shown in Fig. 3(c and d). The maximum potential of the rigidly constrained energy harvester is much higher than that of the cantilever constrained, reaching 142 V. However, since the two similar positive and negative potentials cancel each other, the electrical output effect is seriously reduced, and the output power is far lower than that of the cantilever constrained energy harvester. Therefore, when the constraint condition is a rigid constraint, certain optimization measures can be taken to improve its electrical output effect according to its positive and negative potential cancellation problem.
(2)
Optimization based on stress distribution

The stress uniformity and durability of each area of the energy harvester are reflected by its stress distribution [20]. Therefore, the finite element method is used to analyze the stress distribution characteristics at 3 Hz, 6 Hz, and 10 Hz frequencies. The stress cloud diagram is shown in Fig. 3(e and f).

It can be seen from Fig. 3 (e) that with the change of excitation frequency, the stress value and distribution of the cantilever constrained energy harvester externally connected with the best load resistance are nearly the same, the stress is concentrated at the fixed end and shows a gradient downward trend with the increase of the distance from the fixed end. The stress at the free end is close to zero, and the utilization rate of piezoelectric materials is extremely low. It can be seen from Fig. 3 (f) that the stress of the rigidly constrained energy harvester is mainly distributed in the middle load application area and the fixed ends on both sides, and is symmetrically distributed. Comparing Fig. 3 (e) and (f), it can be seen that the stress distribution uniformity of the rigidly constrained energy harvester is better than that of the cantilever constrained, and the utilization rate of piezoelectric materials is higher. More piezoelectric materials can be excited under the same excitation conditions, but the stress value of the rigidly constrained energy harvester is much higher than that of the cantilever constrained. The reason is that the greater deformation limit of the rigidly constrained energy harvester leads to higher stress.

To sum up, the cantilever constrained energy harvester has the characteristics of low potential, low stress, high electrical output, and poor stress distribution uniformity. When applied, the stress uniformity of energy harvesters should be improved, to improve the utilization rate of piezoelectric materials and the electrical output effect of energy harvesters. The rigidly constrained energy harvester has the characteristics of high potential, high stress, and low electrical output. When applied, the energy harvester can be optimized from the perspective of fixed end and displacement application area to reduce deformation constraint or avoid piezoelectric ceramics covering to the maximum stress, reduce the stress value of energy harvester, and improve the electrical output effect. Therefore, the structural optimization of the rigidly constrained energy harvester is studied.

When the energy harvester is rigidly constrained, the stress and strain are concentrated at the end. A small distance between the piezoelectric layer and the fixed end can make the end of the piezoelectric layer avoid the position of large stress. To clarify the effect of the distance between the piezoelectric layer and the fixed end on the mechanical-electrical performance, the electrical output and stress distribution characteristics are studied when the piezoelectric layer is 0–7.0 mm away from the fixed end.
(1)
Distance analysis based on electrical output

To clarify the influence of the distance between the piezoelectric layer and the fixed end on the electrical output effect of the rigidly constrained energy harvester, a 10 Hz-1.0 mm sinusoidal displacement excitation is applied to the force carrier. The output power under different distances between the piezoelectric layer and the fixed end is shown in Fig. 4 (a and b). When the distance is less than 3.0 mm, the output power gradually increases with the increase of the distance. When the distance increases from 0 to 1.0 mm, the maximum power increases by nearly a thousand times. With further increase of the distance, the maximum output power gradually increases and the amplitude gradually decreases. When the distance is 3.0 mm, the output power reaches a peak of 208 mW. When the distance increases further, the output power decreases slightly. Therefore, when the distance between the piezoelectric layer and the fixed end is 3.0 mm, the rigidly constrained piezoelectric energy harvester has the best electrical output effect.
(2)
Distance based on stress distribution

Apply a displacement load of 10 Hz-1.0 mm to the force transmission carrier. To be closer to the practical application effect of the energy harvester, external matching resistance is connected. The stress distribution of the energy harvester is shown in Fig. 4 (c). With the increase in the distance between the piezoelectric layer and the fixed end, the stress increases first and then decreases. When the distance increases from 0 to 1.0 mm, the maximum stress surges to 692 MPa. With the further increase of the distance, the stress gradually decreases. When the distance increases to 7.0 mm, it decreases to 245 MPa. From the perspective of stress, the distance of the energy harvester should be as large as possible to improve its durability.

To further optimize the distance between the piezoelectric layer and the fixed end of the energy harvester, the electrical output effect and stress distribution are comprehensively considered, and the variation law of the maximum power and the maximum stress value is drawn (Fig. 4 (d)). The energy harvesters for roads are required to have a high electrical output effect and low-stress value. When the distance increases from 5.0 mm to 6.0 mm, the maximum power reduction rate is higher than the maximum stress reduction rate, indicating that increasing the distance between the piezoelectric layer and the fixed end will lead to the stress gain being lower than the power loss. Therefore, when the distance is 5.0 mm, the overall stress level can be effectively reduced while ensuring good electrical output. At this time, the maximum output power is 196.786 mW, and the maximum stress value is 270 MPa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization based on displacement application region
ce:section.ce:para:
When both ends of the energy harvester are rigidly constrained, a certain displacement excitation is applied in the middle of the energy harvester, and the displacement application area bears large stress and strain. The direction of the stress and strain is opposite to that of the end, resulting in the mutual cancellation of the charges generated by the piezoelectric materials. Therefore, the piezoelectric layer is separated from the left and right. The total area of the piezoelectric layer is kept unchanged, the length of the substrate is extended, and the distance between the left and right piezoelectric layers is kept at a certain distance, to clarify the electrical output effect and stress distribution characteristics of the energy harvester under different distances. Since the variation of the horizontal distance between the two piezoelectric layers affects the application of the displacement excitation of the cylindrical force carrier, the method of changing the diameter of the force carrier with the horizontal distance is adopted for analysis.
(1)
Optimization based on electrical output

To clarify the influence of the horizontal distance between the piezoelectric layers on the electrical output effect, a sinusoidal displacement excitation of 10 Hz-1.0 mm is applied to the force carrier. The output power of energy harvesters under different piezoelectric layer distances is shown in Fig. 5 (a and b). Dividing the piezoelectric layer into left and right blocks can effectively improve the electrical output effect. With the increase of the horizontal distance between the left and right piezoelectric layers, the output power increases first and then decreases, and the optimal load resistance is stable between 70–80 kΩ. When the distance of the energy harvester is 1.0 mm, the maximum power increases nearly a thousand times compared with that before blocking. When the distance is further increased, the power gradually increases and the increased amplitude gradually decreases. When the distance is 6.0 mm, the power reaches a peak of 207.04 mW. When the distance is more than 6.0 mm, the power decreases with the increase in the distance. Therefore, when the distance between the piezoelectric layers is 6.0 mm, the best electrical output effect of the energy harvester can be guaranteed.
(2)
Optimization based on stress distribution

To clarify the influence law of the distance between the piezoelectric layers on the stress distribution, a sinusoidal displacement excitation of 10 Hz-1.0 mm is applied to the force transmission carrier. The energy harvester is externally connected with a resistance matching the resistance value. The stress distribution of the energy harvester is shown in Fig. 5 (c). When the left and right piezoelectric layers are separated, with the increase of the distance between the left and right piezoelectric layers, the maximum stress increases first and then decreases and is distributed in the displacement application area. When the distance increases from 0 mm to 1.0 mm, the stress rapidly increases to 1080 MPa. With the distance increasing further, the stress gradually decreases, and when increases to 7.0 mm, it decreases to 319 MPa. From the stress point of view, if the energy harvester adopts the left-right block optimization method, the horizontal distance between the two piezoelectric layers should be as large as possible to reduce the stress level.

To further optimize the distance between the piezoelectric layers, the electrical output effect, and the stress distribution are comprehensively considered. The variation law of the maximum output power and the maximum stress value is drawn (Fig. 5 (d)). When the distance is 6.0 mm, the power of the energy harvester reaches the peak value. The power and stress decrease to a certain extent when the distance further increases. Therefore, the distance between the left and right piezoelectric layers is 7.0 mm, which can ensure high-efficiency electrical energy output and effective stress reduction. At this time, the maximum output power is 200.06 mW, and the maximum stress is 319 MPa.

To sum up, when the constraint form of the cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester is the rigid constraint, increasing the distance between the piezoelectric layer and the fixed end or dividing the piezoelectric layer into left and right blocks can effectively improve the electrical output effect and reduce the stress value. The maximum output power obtained by the two optimization methods is similar, but the stress value under the former optimization method is lower. Therefore, the rigidly constrained energy harvester can improve its mechanical-electrical performance by increasing the distance between the piezoelectric layer and the fixed end.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size design based on mechanical-electrical performance

ce:section.ce:para: The thickness of the piezoelectric layer and substrate has a great influence on the stress value of the energy harvester. The thinner piezoelectric layer and substrate help to reduce stress and improve the electrical output effect [21]. Therefore, the thickness of the selected substrate and piezoelectric layer should be as low as possible. As the main bearing part of the energy harvester, the thickness of the substrate should not be too thin. The thickness of the selected substrate and piezoelectric layer is 0.2 mm. To further optimize the size of the energy harvester, when the width of the piezoelectric layer is 30 mm, the influence of the length of the piezoelectric layer on the electrical output and stress distribution is studied by using the finite element method; When the length is 30 mm, the influence of the width on the electrical output and stress distribution is studied. The effect of length and width on the mechanical-electrical performance is shown in Fig. 6 .

As can be seen from Fig. 6 (a), with the increase of the length, the output power, the substrate stress, and the stress of the piezoelectric layer gradually decrease. Therefore, properly increasing the length can effectively reduce the stress of the piezoelectric layer and the substrate, but it will also reduce the output power. As can be seen from Fig. 6 (b), with the increase in width, the output power tends to increase nearly linearly, the stress of the piezoelectric layer slightly decreases, and the stress of the substrate slightly increases. Therefore, properly increasing the width can effectively improve the output power while maintaining the stability of the piezoelectric layer and substrate stress.

The limit stress of piezoelectric ceramics is relatively low (<100 MPa) [22]. After introducing the conventional safety factor, the stress of piezoelectric ceramics should not be higher than 67 MPa. To ensure that the energy harvester does not undergo fatigue damage under multiple frequency actions, combined with the conclusion that the distance between the piezoelectric layer and the fixed end affects the stress of the energy harvester in Section 3.2, the stress of the piezoelectric layer is maintained at 60–67 MPa by adjusting the distance. The width of the piezoelectric layer is tentatively 20 mm and the width of the fixed end is 10 mm. The power density, the stress of the piezoelectric layer, and the substrate stress when the length of the piezoelectric layer is 10–40 mm are shown in Fig. 7 .

As can be seen from Fig. 7(a and b), under the same piezoelectric layer stress level, with the increase of the length of the piezoelectric layer, the terminal voltage gradually decreases, the maximum output power gradually increases, and the power density and the substrate stress gradually decrease. Therefore, although the increase of the length of the piezoelectric layer can effectively reduce the substrate stress and improve the output power, the utilization efficiency of piezoelectric materials drops sharply, and the economic efficiency of the energy harvester decreases seriously.

To further clarify the optimal length of the energy harvester, the terminal voltage, maximum output power, and power density are taken as indicators, and the principal component analysis method is selected to convert multiple indicators into comprehensive electrical indicators composed of different weight indicators. The results of the principal component analysis are shown in Table 2 . The contribution rate of principal component 1 is as high as 97.85%. Therefore, each eigenvector of principal component 1 is selected to calculate the weight, and the terminal voltage, maximum output power, and power density eigenvectors of principal component 1 are 0.994, −0.980, and 0.994 respectively, and the weights are 98.649%, −97.251% and 98.602% respectively. The fatigue strength of the metal substrate material is 200 MPa [23]. As can be seen from Fig. 7 (b), when the piezoelectric layer length is less than 20 mm, the substrate stress does not meet the requirements. Therefore, the relationship between the length and the comprehensive electrical index when the piezoelectric layer is longer than 20 mm is analyzed (Fig. 7 (c)), and the best length of the piezoelectric layer is selected. When the length of the piezoelectric layer is 20 mm, the comprehensive electrical index value of the energy harvester is the highest and tends to decrease with the increase of the length. Based on this, the width of the energy harvester is further optimized based on the 20 mm length of the piezoelectric layer and the 70 mm total length of the energy harvester.

Since the size of piezoelectric ceramics rarely exceeds 60 mm, further increasing the size will lead to a rapid increase in the processing difficulty. Therefore, the performance of the energy harvester is shown in Fig. 8 when the width is 20–60 mm.

As can be seen from Fig. 8 (a), when the length of the piezoelectric layer is 20 mm, the terminal voltage and maximum output power gradually increase with the increase of the width, and the improvement effect is significant. The maximum power density also increases, but the amplitude gradually decreases. It is shown that the electrical output improvement effect decreases gradually with the increase of the width, and the electrical output effect tends to be maximized. As can be seen from Fig. 8 (b), when the width is less than 30 mm, the substrate stress slightly increases with the increase of the width. When the width is greater than 30 mm, the substrate stress gradually tends to be stable with the increase of the width. With the increase of the width, the stress of the piezoelectric layer gradually decreases and then tends to be stable when the width is about 40 mm. Therefore, the width should be greater than 40 mm to ensure that the stress of the piezoelectric layer is at a low value.

Considering the electrical performance and stress value comprehensively, the energy harvester with a width of 60 mm has both high electrical output performance and low piezoelectric layer stress. Therefore, the width of the energy harvester is recommended to be 60 mm. The size of the piezoelectric layer is 20 mm × 60 mm × 0.2 mm, and the substrate size is 70 mm × 60 mm × 0.2 mm.

According to the finite element simulation results in Section 3, the stress distribution uniformity of the rigidly constrained energy harvester is better than that of the cantilever constraint, and the utilization rate of the piezoelectric material is higher. Under the same excitation conditions, more power benefits can be obtained. After increasing the distance between the piezoelectric layer and the fixed end, the electrical output effect of the rigidly constrained energy harvester can be effectively improved and is significantly higher than that of the cantilever constraint. To verify the excellent electrical output characteristics of the cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester with rigid constraint, the output voltage and output power are tested, and the MTS Landmark was used to simulate the road traffic load.

To accurately analyze the electrical output law of the energy harvester, take the actuation displacement of 1.0 mm as an example [24], connect resistors with different resistance values in series with the energy harvester, and measure the voltage value of the external resistance with the digital oscilloscope. The terminal voltage is shown in Fig. 9 (a). When the actuation displacement is 1.0 mm, the terminal voltage increases with the increase of the actuation frequency, and the increased amplitude is obvious at low frequency. The peak voltage is 8.20 V at a 2 Hz action frequency. With the increase of loading frequency, the peak voltage at each frequency increases by 85.37% (15.20 V), 18.42% (18.00 V), and 33.33% (24.00 V) respectively, indicating that the electrical performance at low actuation frequencies is difficult to give full play to. The application planning of energy harvesters can be focused on road sections with a rolling frequency higher than 6 Hz.

The voltage under the optimum load refers to the voltage value corresponding to the load when its output power reaches the maximum. To ensure that the energy harvester outputs more electric energy under the same operating conditions, the external resistance value should be maintained within a certain range, so that the power harvested by the external load is close to the peak value. Therefore, the practical application of energy harvesters needs to study the variation of resistance and voltage. The voltage under the optimum load is shown in Fig. 9 (b). When the actuation displacement is 1.0 mm, the voltage under optimum load increases with the increase of the actuation frequency. The voltages under optimum load at different actuation frequencies are 4.56 V, 8.20 V, 10.80 V, and 14.80 V respectively, which indicate that when the voltage required by external power facilities is high, the energy harvester can be applied to the fast lane to improve its voltage under optimum load and meet the voltage demand of external facilities.

To sum up, the terminal voltage and the voltage under optimum load increase with the increase of the actuation frequency. To improve the current flow performance of the road piezoelectric power generation system, the energy harvester can be applied to the highway at high speed.

The output power can further reflect its electrical output effect. The output power of the energy harvester is shown in Fig. 9 (c). The output power increases rapidly at first and then decreases gradually with the increase of the resistance value of the external load. There is an optimum load to make the output power reach the peak. At this time, the output voltage is the above voltage under optimum load. When the dynamic displacement is 1.0 mm, the maximum output power increases with the increase of the loading frequency and the effect is obvious, which increases 706.47% (0.982 mW), 108.12% (1.212 mW) and 87.78% (2.048 mW) respectively. When the dynamic frequency increases from 2 Hz to 6 Hz, the maximum output power increases significantly. Therefore, the energy harvester should be applied to the road section with a rolling frequency greater than 6 Hz to ensure its high-efficiency electric energy output.

The power density represents the piezoelectric material utilization efficiency to a certain extent. The energy harvester with high power density often represents its high performance and performance-price ratio. The power output density is shown in Fig. 9 (d). The maximum power density increases with the increase of the loading frequency, and the improvement effect is obvious at a high loading frequency. Under 12 Hz-1.0 mm, the power density of the energy harvester is as high as 9126.67 W/m3, and its electrical performance is remarkable.

To sum up, the increase in the loading frequency helps to improve the output power and power density of the energy harvester. The electrical output effect is significant under various actuation conditions, which reflects the excellent electrical performance of the designed energy harvester. On the other hand, the application planning of energy harvesters can focus on the layout of fast lanes.

To further analyze the superiority of the electrical performance of the piezoelectric energy harvester proposed in this paper, the latest research progress of typical cantilever piezoelectric energy harvesters is comprehensively investigated. Due to the different piezoelectric materials, structural types, constraint forms, loading conditions, and sizes of energy harvesters studied, the electrical output effects are also different. Three electrical indexes of output voltage, output power, and power density are selected to comprehensively compare and evaluate the electrical performance to illustrate the effectiveness of the results of this study.

Under the frequency range of road engineering, the output voltage of the energy harvester proposed in this paper is 24 V under the loading condition of 12 Hz-1.0 mm, the output power is 4.38 mW, and the power density is 9126.67 W/m3. Its output voltage is higher than that of the energy harvester under the displacement excitation of 2.5 mm [25], the output power is higher than that at the same loading frequency [16], and the power density is higher than that under the displacement excitation of 5 mm [14]. Therefore, its power generation performance is better than that of most cantilever piezoelectric energy harvesters. Compared with the research results that exceed the working frequency of road engineering, the output power and power density of this paper are higher than those of energy harvesters at 43.7 Hz and 60 Hz loading frequencies [26,27], and the electrical output effect is remarkable.

In addition, in terms of test conditions, the action displacement of the cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester in this paper is significantly smaller than that of other studies, which is more in line with the rolling characteristics of road engineering. It is further proved that the optimization method proposed in this paper can effectively improve the electrical output effect. The electrical performance is better than most cantilever piezoelectric energy harvesters, which verifies that the energy harvester structure with high electrical output and better stress distribution is suitable for road engineering applications.

In summary, the cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester structure is optimized according to the electrical output and stress distribution characteristics, the energy harvester size with high electrical output and better stress distribution is optimized, and the good electrical effect of the proposed energy harvester structure and size is verified, which solves the problem that the current cantilever piezoelectric energy harvester has a low electrical output level and a low matching degree with road traffic. In this paper, by selecting the distance between the piezoelectric layer and the fixed end to be 5.0 mm, the electrical output effect is improved based on reducing the stress value. The output voltage, output power, and power density of the proposed energy harvester are 24 V, 4.38 mW, and 9126.67 W/m3 under the test conditions of 12 Hz-1.0 mm, which can meet the application requirements of road piezoelectric power generation.

In this paper, the structural characteristics of cantilever piezoelectric energy harvesters under different constraint forms are analyzed based on the finite element model. The structure of the rigidly constrained energy harvester is optimized by increasing the distance between the piezoelectric layer and the fixed end and dividing the piezoelectric layer into blocks. The size of the piezoelectric energy harvester with high electrical output and better stress distribution is optimized. The electrical output performance of the proposed energy harvester is systematically tested. Finally, the electrical effect under the optimal structure and size is compared and evaluated. The electrical output index is higher than most other cantilever piezoelectric energy harvesters. The research results provide a solution for cantilever piezoelectric energy harvesters to meet the force-electrical performance requirements in the road fields and help to promote the application of road piezoelectric power generation technology. In the future, road piezoelectric power generation devices and energy acquisition and storage systems that meet the actual application requirements can be further developed, to provide a basis for the laying and monitoring of road piezoelectric power generation devices.

Chaohui Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Ruoling Zhou: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Shuai Wang: Methodology, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Huazhi Yuan: Investigation, Project administration, Writing – review. Hongyun Cao: Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This paper describes research activities mainly requested and sponsored by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, China (No. 300102212910), Innovation Capability Support Program of Shaanxi (No. 2022TD-07) and “Hongliu Excellent Young” Talents Support Program of Lanzhou University of Technology, China. The sponsorship and team's efforts are gratefully acknowledged.
